title: Cat Inability to Retract Claws: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your cat can't retract their claws. Learn about urgency levels, causes, and how providing a photo can help your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: A cat’s inability to retract their claws is typically a low-urgency condition caused by overgrown nails, aging, or ligament issues, but requires veterinary care if accompanied by bleeding or pain.

Why is my cat unable to retract its claws?

  • Claws stay extended when the elastic ligaments that normally hold them in a retracted position are damaged.
  • Nails may become too thick or overgrown, preventing the claw from being pulled back properly.
  • Other potential causes include neurological issues, senior age, or infections of the nail bed.

Is it an emergency if my cat cannot retract its claws?

  • The urgency level for this condition is generally Low and is not typically a life-threatening situation.
  • Extended claws can be uncomfortable and may get caught on fabric or carpet, leading to further injury.
  • Contact your veterinarian during regular business hours if you notice accompanying symptoms like heavy bleeding, a foul odor from the paw, or a sudden inability to walk.

How does a photo help my veterinarian triage this claw issue?

  • A high-resolution photo allows a professional to see the exact angle of the claw and the condition of the surrounding skin.
  • Visual information helps identify signs of swelling, discharge, or infection.
  • This helps your vet determine if the issue is a simple matter of overgrowth requiring a trim or a more complex medical issue requiring an in-person examination.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, the inability to retract claws can be attributed to several factors. Genetic defects, such as the autosomal dominant "lobster claw" defect, can cause digit contractures, digit aplasia, and metacarpal hypoplasia or fusion, affecting claw retraction. Additionally, rupture of the flexor tendon can lead to dorsal rotation of the toe.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1077)

faqs.question: Can senior cats lose the ability to retract their claws?
faqs.answer: Yes, senior cats often experience thickening of the nails and a decrease in muscle or ligament elasticity, which makes full retraction more difficult.

faqs.question: Is it painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: The inability to retract the claw itself may not be painful, but the underlying cause—such as an infection, injury, or overgrown nail piercing the pad—can cause significant discomfort.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat gets stuck on furniture?
faqs.answer: Carefully help them unhook the claw without pulling. If this happens frequently, it is a sign that their nails need trimming or a veterinary evaluation is necessary.
